Python mirrors the Coq spec: empty text requires result None; non-empty text requires result be the fixed hash. Using None for None and a bare string for Some hash yields the same truth judgment on all inputs.
Both require result to be None when text is empty, and to equal the fixed hash for any non-empty text (with Some in Coq mapped to a plain string in Python).
Both require result=None when text is empty, and require result to be exactly the fixed string '3e25960a79dbc69b674cd4ec67a72c62' when text is non-empty, modeling option as None-or-string.
